name: @chenglou/pretext
summary: Fast multiline text measurement and layout without DOM reflow

story.problem: Measuring text height in the browser requires querying the DOM — calling getBoundingClientRect() or reading offsetHeight forces the browser to perform a full layout reflow, one of the most expensive operations possible. In virtualised lists, dynamic text containers, or layouts where text flows around obstacles, you may need thousands of these measurements per frame. DOM-based measurement makes that cost prohibitive.
story.mental_model: Pretext separates the expensive part (measuring character widths via Canvas) from the cheap part (computing line breaks via arithmetic). Call prepare() once per text string — it measures every segment with Canvas and caches the results. Then call layout() as many times as you like at any width, entirely in arithmetic, with no DOM access at all. Prepare once, layout many times.
story.when_to_use: Use pretext when you need to know text dimensions before rendering — virtualised scrolling lists where you must know item heights upfront, text flowing around images or arbitrary obstacles, dynamic subtitle/caption positioning, detecting content overflow at build time, or any animation that requires text metrics on every frame.
story.when_not_to_use: Pretext only measures plain text — it cannot handle HTML markup, inline images, or mixed rich content. For simple cases where you measure a single element once on mount, the browser's built-in ResizeObserver or getBoundingClientRect is simpler and sufficient. Pretext's two-phase API adds conceptual overhead that is only justified when measurements are frequent or happen before render.

alternatives.name: ResizeObserver
alternatives.reason: Built-in browser API that notifies you when an element changes size — simpler when you just need to react to size changes after render rather than predict them before

alternatives.name: canvas measureText
alternatives.reason: The raw Canvas API that pretext uses internally — reach for it directly for one-off measurements, but you lose pretext's caching, line-break logic, and multilingual handling

alternatives.name: opentype.js
alternatives.reason: Parses font files for full glyph path access — use it when you need to render text to SVG paths or manipulate letterforms, not just measure layout

graph_nodes.label: prepare()
graph_nodes.summary: One-time text preparation — measures every segment's width via Canvas and returns an opaque PreparedText handle. This is the expensive step (~19ms for 500 texts); do it once and reuse the handle.
graph_nodes.signature: prepare(text: string, font: string, options?: Options): PreparedText

graph_nodes.label: prepareWithSegments()
graph_nodes.summary: Like prepare() but also returns the raw segments array, enabling custom rendering or inspection of individual text chunks.
graph_nodes.signature: prepareWithSegments(text: string, font: string, options?: Options): { prepared: PreparedText; segments: Segment[] }

graph_nodes.label: layout()
graph_nodes.summary: Fast height and line count calculation from a PreparedText handle at a given max width. Runs in ~0.09ms — no DOM access, pure arithmetic on cached segment widths.
graph_nodes.signature: layout(prepared: PreparedText, maxWidth: number, lineHeight: number): { height: number; lineCount: number }

graph_nodes.label: layoutWithLines()
graph_nodes.summary: Like layout() but also returns per-line details — the text content and pixel width of each line. Use this when you need to position or style individual lines.
graph_nodes.signature: layoutWithLines(prepared: PreparedText, maxWidth: number, lineHeight: number): { height: number; lineCount: number; lines: Line[] }

graph_nodes.label: layoutNextLine()
graph_nodes.summary: Incremental single-line layout from a character cursor position — returns the next line's text range and width. Use this for streaming or obstacle-aware layouts where each line has a different available width.
graph_nodes.signature: layoutNextLine(prepared: PreparedText, startPos: number, maxWidth: number): { endPos: number; width: number } | null

graph_nodes.label: walkLineRanges()
graph_nodes.summary: Iterates over every line range in the text, calling a callback with start position, end position, and line width. Efficient for building custom rendering pipelines.
graph_nodes.signature: walkLineRanges(prepared: PreparedText, maxWidth: number, callback: (start: number, end: number, width: number) => void): void

graph_nodes.label: clearCache()
graph_nodes.summary: Releases all cached Canvas font measurements from memory. Call this when you've finished a batch of measurements and want to free memory.

graph_nodes.label: setLocale()
graph_nodes.summary: Configures the locale used by Intl.Segmenter for text boundary detection. Affects where line breaks occur in CJK and other non-Latin scripts.

graph_nodes.label: PreparedText
graph_nodes.summary: Opaque handle returned by prepare(). Stores cached segment widths and font metrics — pass it to layout functions. Treat it as immutable; do not inspect its internals.

steps.label: Prepare the text once
steps.explanation: prepare() measures every word segment via Canvas and caches the widths. This is the only expensive step — do it once per string.

steps.label: Compute height at any width — no DOM needed
steps.explanation: layout() runs entirely in arithmetic — it checks cached widths against maxWidth to find line breaks. You can call it thousands of times per frame safely.

steps.label: Reuse the handle at different widths
steps.explanation: Because the handle is immutable, you can call layout() at as many widths as you need without re-measuring.

steps.label: Use layoutWithLines for line-level detail
steps.explanation: layoutWithLines returns the same height and lineCount as layout(), plus a lines array. Each entry has the text content and pixel width of that line.

steps.label: Position elements using line widths
steps.explanation: Because you know each line's width ahead of time, you can centre, right-align, or justify lines without DOM queries.

steps.label: Use layoutNextLine to vary width per line
steps.explanation: layoutNextLine gives you one line at a time from a character cursor, letting you change the available width on every line. This is how you implement text flowing around shapes, images, or arbitrary obstacles.

tasks.title: Pre-calculate heights for a virtualised list

steps.label: Prepare all items upfront
steps.explanation: In a virtualised list you need row heights before you can render any rows. Pretext lets you compute all of them upfront in a fast batch, then pass the heights array to your virtualiser (react-window, tanstack-virtual, etc.).

steps.label: Pass heights to a virtualiser
steps.explanation: react-window's VariableSizeList accepts an itemSize function. Because you pre-calculated all heights with pretext, there's no layout thrashing during scroll.
